# FULL-POWER POLARIS — End-to-End Architecture (to beat Gemini 3.1 Pro DR + ChatGPT 5.5 Pro DR)

Document class: Architecture, Codex-gated, project-canonical.
Date: 2026-05-31. Author role: Chief Architect (synthesis of 4 upstream architect deliverables + design anchors).
Scope: pipeline A (honest-rebuild), full-power configuration. Pipeline B (UI) and C (frozen) governed separately per `architecture.md`.

## 0. Purpose, runtime, and the wedge

**Goal.** Design FULL-POWER POLARIS end-to-end — every stage — engineered to BEAT Gemini 3.1 Pro DR and ChatGPT 5.5 Pro DR on the 5 golden clinical questions, scored claim-by-claim faithfulness (RACE / FACT) plus gold-rubric coverage ≥ 0.70. We win on **auditable faithfulness**, not length.

**Runtime (locked, open-weight OpenRouter lineup — `outputs/I-meta-002/role_selection_final.md:32-35`):**

- **Writer** — `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` (MIT, 1M ctx, GDPval-AA 1558 Elo #1 open-weight). The only role that authors text; runs all front-half query/plan/synthesis work.
- **Mirror** — `z-ai/glm-5.1` (MIT, AA-Omniscience 29.4% hallucination — best-calibrated in eligible pool). Calibration + conflict surfacing.
- **Sentinel** — `ibm-granite/granite-guardian-4.1-8b` (Apache-2.0, self-host, RAGTruth BAcc 0.841 think / 0.834 non-think). The lethal faithfulness role; self-hosted so no US-vendor inference touches the verification path (`feedback_sovereignty_threat_model_2026_05_13`).
- **Judge** — `qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b` (Apache-2.0). Terminal per-claim 5-enum verdict; distinct lineage from Writer (two-family invariant, §9.1.1).

Four distinct training families. Cost target ≈ **$2.63/run** (reconciled in §5).

**The wedge (do NOT trade it away).** Per-sentence provenance `[#ev:<id>:<start>-<end>]` + 4-role verification + fail-closed refusal. Frontier fabricates and cites loosely: Gemini's sovereign-compute report is **4,932 words with zero citations** (`state/compare_gemini_q1.md`); ChatGPT stacks nine footnote indices on one paragraph (`state/compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:1067-1075`) and uses opaque `turn31view0` tokens that resolve to nothing (`state/compare_chatgpt_q1.md:11`). POLARIS makes every shipped number one click from its proof, and shows what it refused.

**Shipped baseline we are closing.** Single-pass retrieval (1 main query + R6 expansion capped at 4, `PG_R6_EXPAND_QUERY_CAP=4`); fetch-cap 40 (`run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1627` / header-correction `:1714`); gen-cap 20 (`MAX_EV_TO_GEN`, `:2266` / `:2340`; `evidence_selector.py:976`); deepener default-off; 4-role gate built but not default-on (`run_one_query` called WITHOUT transport at `:3940`, `corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md:12-13`); ~30% verified prose. The full-power design closes every one.

**Honesty up front (per `corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md` Codex header corrections 1-4):** the relevance-driven corpus, map-reduce, saturation stop-rule, and default-on 4-role gate are **designed, not yet built** — fixed caps are still live in code. The "~120-200 relevant of ~1,000 discovered" density is an **unmeasured prior** (extrapolated from TREC-2020 ~29% top-rank precision, `corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md:91`). No "beat" is proven until a blinded golden-5 run scores claim-by-claim. The wedge is structurally sound; its measured margin is a hypothesis pending that run.

---

## 1. END-TO-END PIPELINE (full-power stage sequence)

**Stage 1 — Scope & decomposition (PICO faceting).** Detects domain (clinical / regulatory / policy), parses the question into Population / Intervention / Comparator / Outcome plus the safety facets clinical RAG requires — contraindications, adverse-event signals, special populations, regulatory-label divergence, comparator head-to-heads — and sets a per-domain `relevance_tau` floor. **Model/tool:** existing scope gate (`src/polaris_graph/nodes/`, §9.2) + Writer for PICO + synonym/MeSH-candidate proposal, validated against the keyless NCBI E-utilities MeSH database so no hallucinated MeSH ID reaches a query. **Fixes vs shipped:** `query_decomposer.py` is pure string-splitting — no LLM, no MeSH, no PICO, no synonyms, hard cap 6 sub-queries (`query_decomposer.py:33-36,108-148`); it cannot expand "tirzepatide"→"Mounjaro" or map "T2DM"→MeSH `Diabetes Mellitus, Type 2`. Full-power replaces flat keyword fan-out with semantic PICO faceting (~20-40 backend-native queries) feeding the existing deterministic expanders (`regulatory_expander.py`, `primary_trial_expander.py`).

**Stage 2 — Research plan (explicit, editable, enforced).** Compiles the PICO plan into an ordered set of report sections with, per section, an **evidence target** (minimum tier mix + minimum distinct findings) and a machine-checkable **definition of done** (each gold-rubric coverage axis backed by ≥1 verified finding, else the section is flagged INCOMPLETE — never silently thin). Rendered in the built editable plan-review UI (tasks #459/#754) so the operator edits scope before any generator token is billed. **Model/tool:** Writer drafts from the scope template (`nodes/scope_gate.load_scope_template`) + `section_blueprint.py`; targets written to the run manifest as a checkable `plan` block. **Fixes vs shipped:** sections today come from a static template with no per-section evidence target and no done-definition; a section that retrieves nothing still renders prose at ~30% verified rate. Full-power makes "done" an enforced per-section contract.

**Stage 3 — Multi-round agentic search (STORM-style, saturation-stopped).** Round 1 fires the full PICO query plan across all backends. Between rounds a **gap-analysis** compares retrieved findings to the per-section targets and PICO facets and emits **targeted follow-up queries for gaps only**. Stop rule is **saturation, not a fixed count**: continue until the new-unique-finding rate drops below ~1 per 20 newly-fetched sources for two consecutive batches (`corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md §4.6`), OR all section targets are met, OR the budget cap binds — and report where it saturated. **Model/tool:** Writer runs gap-analysis + follow-up query generation; backends = Serper + Semantic Scholar + Europe PMC (existing, `domain_backends.py:331`) + **new first-class PubMed E-utilities (esearch/efetch, keyless)** + **new first-class ClinicalTrials.gov v2 API**. **Fixes vs shipped:** today is effectively single-pass + one bolt-on R6 expansion capped at 4 queries (`run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1885-1891`) with no gap-analysis and no saturation rule; PubMed is treated as "already well-served" via generic Serper (`domain_backends.py:7`) and ClinicalTrials.gov is **skipped** (`:452`). Full-power adds the two clinical-channel backends and a true gap-driven loop.

**Stage 4 — Relevance + tier-driven fetch (no count cap; `corpus_funnel`).** Fetches every candidate that clears all three bars and drops the rest **with a counted reason**: (1) tier bar (T1-T4 admissible; T5/T6 only with a regulatory-content marker; hard-drop T7 stubs/predatory via `tier_classifier.py`); (2) relevance bar (`_row_relevance ≥ tau`, tau from the score-distribution knee, floored by `PG_RELEVANCE_TAU_FLOOR`, adaptive — never constant); (3) dedup-by-finding (collapse same-trial/DOI-family sources to one FINDING with N provenance pointers). Emits the **binding `corpus_funnel` invariant** to the manifest: `discovered / dropped_tier / dropped_relevance / dropped_dedup / kept=N / tau`. **Model/tool:** `live_retriever` fetch loop with the `chosen[:fetch_cap]` truncation (`live_retriever.py:1573-1591`) replaced by a relevance/tier floor; `evidence_selector.select_evidence_for_generation` (`evidence_selector.py:976`) with `max_rows` truncation removed; no new model — selection logic only. **Fixes vs shipped:** this is the central loophole — the pipeline DISCOVERS ~1,000 candidates but DUMPS ~96% at two fixed-COUNT cuts (FETCH_CAP=40 then MAX_EV_TO_GEN=20) by POSITION not quality, and the dump is SILENT (drops go to a best-effort Path-B trace, not a counted manifest funnel) (`corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md:37`). Keeping ALL 1,000 would hurt (lost-in-the-middle); the goal is the **covering set** (~120-200, an unmeasured prior to MEASURE), reported by count.

**Stage 5 — Relevance corpus → per-source MAP digest.** One independent DeepSeek digest call per kept source → a structured claim record (≈2,500 tok in / 500 out), embarrassingly parallel. **Model/tool:** new `evidence_reducer` (`corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md §4.2`) + Writer. **Fixes vs shipped:** replaces the cram-then-truncate single-context approach; unbinds N from the 1M window and avoids lost-in-the-middle degradation (Liu 2307.03172, Levy 2503.04388). Each source is read in isolation, so no source is diluted by neighbours.

**Stage 6 — Dedup-to-FINDINGS + hierarchical REDUCE synthesis.** Clusters digests into unique findings-with-N-provenance, then synthesizes section-by-section **over the digests** (≈500 tok each), not over raw source text. **Model/tool:** `multi_section_generator` re-pointed at findings (`corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md §4.3-4.4`) + Writer. **Fixes vs shipped:** lifts the ~30% verified-prose ceiling — the 30% is a SUPPLY problem (only 20 evidence rows reach the writer, `:2266`), so the writer runs out of grounded material mid-report; more verified supply → higher verified fraction at the same coverage. Honest breadth: a meta-analysis + 5 press rehashes collapse to 1 finding + 6 provenance pointers (frontier counts them as 5 sources of apparent breadth).

**Stage 7 — Generation with per-sentence provenance.** Every generated sentence carries a `[#ev:<evidence_id>:<start>-<end>]` token, and generation is **evidence-first**: composed FROM findings that already carry provenance, so a sentence with no backing finding is not written in the first place (verified-by-construction, not verified-by-post-hoc-patch). **Model/tool:** Writer + `generator/provenance` (§9.1.2). **Fixes vs shipped:** the token mechanism exists but operates at ~30% rate; map-reduce raises the grounded fraction. **The inversion is the composition fix:** frontier writes the narrative then attaches citations (hence Gemini's zero-anchor q1 and ChatGPT's end-stacked footnotes); POLARIS writes only what a finding supports.

**Stage 8 — 4-role verification (per-claim terminal verdict).** Per atomized claim: **atomize** (Writer splits a sentence with two numbers into two claim-records sharing one span — finer than shipped sentence-level strict_verify) → **Mirror** 2-pass calibration (re-read claim blind to Writer confidence; flag over-claim, emit `calibration_delta`) → **Sentinel** binary RAG-faithfulness gate (grounded y/n; `UNGROUNDED` is **fail-closed** — cannot proceed regardless of Judge) → **Sentinel** qualitative-negation pass (`(did not|no|never|absent|without)+endpoint` must be asserted in the span) → **Judge** terminal 5-enum **VERIFIED / PARTIAL / UNSUPPORTED / FABRICATED / UNREACHABLE** (may downgrade, may NOT upgrade a Sentinel-UNGROUNDED claim) → **python strict_verify** deterministic floor (evidence-id in pool, span bounds valid, every decimal appears verbatim in the span, ≥2 content-word overlap; any failure → DROP, no model override) → **guarded citation-repair** only for span-locality failures (right source, wrong offset): Writer proposes a narrower span from the SAME source, re-judged against the short span; still failing → fail-closed DROP; repair can never invent a source or widen scope. **Model/tool:** `role_pipeline.py` Mirror/Sentinel/Judge (`corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md §4.5`); `generator/strict_verify` (§9.1.3) + M-45 refetch; `openrouter_client.check_family_segregation` (§9.1.1). **Fixes vs shipped:** wires the built-but-not-default gate into the default CLI/UI/queue path (`:3940`); adds atomization, the 5-enum, the negation pass, and the guarded repair (shipped has none — a correct claim with a slightly-off offset is dropped outright, costing coverage). The guarded repair is the corrected design from `project_complete_solution_codex_corrections_2026_05_30` (whole-doc widening would smuggle ungrounded claims; candidate-generator-only + re-judge short span + fail-closed is the safe form).

**Stage 9 — Composition + contradiction (surface conflicts, refuse to adjudicate).** Detects directional conflicts across deduped findings (one source asserts a clinical signal present, another absent, for the same endpoint+population) and **closes the shipped loop**: conflicts become a generation INPUT (Writer must disclose both sides, cite both spans, refuse to adjudicate) AND an evaluation GATE (the evaluator FAILS a report that hides a known directional conflict). Unverified "analyst voice" connective tissue is quarantined to a minimized, clearly-labeled Analysis block so it can never masquerade as a verified claim. **Model/tool:** Mirror (conflict detection) + Writer (conflict-as-input) + Judge (disclosure gate) + contradiction/refusal panel UI (task #454). **Fixes vs shipped:** today qualitative conflicts are written to `contradictions.json` but never fed back into generation or evaluation — they sit inert on disk. Full-power consumes them.

**Stage 10 — Output + audit trail (the category frontier does not contest).** Emits: (O1) a per-sentence VERIFIED/PARTIAL/UNSUPPORTED/FABRICATED/UNREACHABLE/DROPPED **ledger** with the cited span, the Mirror/Sentinel/Judge signals, and the drop-reason — a first-class artifact, not a debug log; (O2) **fail-closed verdict** — if every section fails strict_verify, `report.md` is a pipeline-verdict artifact `abort_no_verified_sections`; rubber-stamp corpus → `abort_corpus_approval_denied` (§9.3); (O3) a **signed audit bundle** (report + ledger + conflict records + `corpus_funnel` + resolved span text, hashed/signed); (O4) the **Proof-Replay split-view** demo centerpiece (click any claim → highlight the exact cited span side-by-side + 4-role verdict chips + decimal-match); (O5) a **saturation/concision report** so concision is auditable, not arbitrary truncation. **Model/tool:** python aggregator over Stage-8 outputs; §9.3 statuses; signed-manifest infra (§9.1); Proof-Replay UI (tasks #448-454, #461). **Fixes vs shipped:** the pipeline emits artifacts but no user-facing ledger, no single signed replayable bundle, and no replay surface; sentences are dropped silently inside strict_verify.

---

## 2. FRONTIER COUNTER-MAP

| Frontier behavior | ChatGPT or Gemini (file evidence) | POLARIS counter (stage / invariant) | wins / parity |
|---|---|---|---|
| No claim→span binding; footnotes stacked at paragraph ends | ChatGPT: nine indices `33-41` on one paragraph (`compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:1067-1075`) | Per-sentence `[#ev:id:start-end]` (Stage 7, §9.1.2) | **WINS** |
| Opaque citation tokens resolving to nothing | ChatGPT: `turn31view0` (`compare_chatgpt_q1.md:11`) | Signed bundle resolves every claim to span text (Stage 10/O3) | **WINS** |
| Whole-report with zero citations | Gemini: 4,932-word q1, no inline markers, no source list (`compare_gemini_q1.md`) | `corpus_funnel` + per-sentence provenance + fail-closed (Stages 4,7,10) | **WINS** |
| Whole-PDF superscript on a precise number | Gemini: "63% … M-value²" → cite 2 = whole Product Monograph (`compare_gemini_dr.txt:57`) | strict_verify: decimal must appear verbatim in the cited span (Stage 8/V7) | **WINS** |
| Figure-read decimals shipped as table values | ChatGPT: 104-wk "6.43/6.13/6.11" read off graphs (`compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:614-616`) | strict_verify DROP + DROPPED ledger entry (Stage 8 + O1) | **WINS** |
| Approximation laundering ("roughly −7.5/−10.7/−12.9 kg") | ChatGPT: `compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:263-266` | Approximated decimal absent from span → fails strict_verify (Stage 8/V7) | **WINS** |
| Fabrication-suspect trial asserted as fact | ChatGPT: "SURPASS-SWITCH, reported in 2026 …" directional conclusion (`compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:1089-1091`) | No citable atom → Judge UNREACHABLE / fail-closed refuse (Stage 8/10) | **WINS** |
| Conflict flattened to a confident bottom-line | ChatGPT: "clearly more potent" while row evidence is "inferential" (`compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:690-700,755-762`) | Mirror conflict-as-input; disclose both sides, refuse to adjudicate (Stage 9) | **WINS** |
| Safety unknown smoothed into a proceed | Gemini: "unknown whether tirzepatide causes MTC in humans" then asserts contraindication (`compare_gemini_dr.txt:541`) | Open-conflict panel; qualitative-negation Sentinel pass (Stage 8/V4, Stage 9) | **WINS** |
| Never refuses / never says "could not verify" | Gemini q1 confident on zero sources (`compare_gemini_q1.md`); ChatGPT never returns "unverifiable" | `abort_no_verified_sections` fail-closed (Stage 10/O2, §9.3) | **WINS** |
| Self-judges (writer = verifier) | Both frontier systems | Two-family segregation, RuntimeError at construction (Stage 8/V6, §9.1.1) | **WINS** |
| Full SURPASS-program per-trial coverage with CIs/p-values | ChatGPT: SURPASS-1..6 + CVOT + SWITCH table (`compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:67-576`) | Multi-round search + PubMed/ctgov backends + no-cap fetch (Stages 3-4) | **PARITY (must hold)** |
| Strong comparison tables | Gemini: SURPASS matrix (`compare_gemini_dr.txt:215-320`); pharmacare matrix (`compare_gemini_q5.md:207-213`) | REDUCE synthesis renders equivalent tables (Stage 6) | **PARITY (must hold)** |
| High-tier source breadth (CMHC/PBO/OSFI/PHARMAC) | Gemini: ~50 sources (`compare_gemini_q2.md:291-528`) | Relevance-driven corpus keeps ≥ as many high-tier sources (Stage 4) | **PARITY** |
| Excellent narrative prose | Gemini: think-tank-grade writing (`compare_gemini_q3.md:85`) | Writer (DeepSeek V4 Pro, GDPval-AA #1 open-weight) must be report-grade (Stage 7) | **PARITY (real risk)** |
| Visible reasoning trace | Gemini: "Thoughts" appendix (`compare_gemini_q2.md:596-602`) | Editable plan (Stage 2) + run-progress/thinking toggle (#452/#754) | **PARITY** |
| Total length / exhaustiveness | ChatGPT 9,289-word q1; Gemini 4,932-word q1 | Saturation stop + report-the-ceiling | **DELIBERATE-BEHIND** |
| Long-term / cross-session memory | Frontier conversational memory | Out of scope for single-shot golden-5 | **DELIBERATE-BEHIND** |

---

## 3. HONEST PER-PARAMETER BEAT / PARITY MAP

Never "beat on all." Length and memory are deliberate not-goals — chasing them dilutes the wedge.

| Parameter | Verdict | One-line reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Faithfulness (claim↔span)** | **BEAT (decisive)** | strict_verify drops any sentence whose decimal isn't verbatim in the cited span (Stage 8/V7); frontier has no claim→span binding at all (`compare_gemini_q1.md` zero citations; `compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:1067-1075` stacked footnotes). |
| **Auditability** | **BEAT (decisive)** | Signed bundle + Proof-Replay click→span→verdict (Stage 10/O3-O4); ChatGPT's `turn31view0` resolves to nothing (`compare_chatgpt_q1.md:11`). |
| **Contradiction-surfacing** | **BEAT** | Conflict-as-input + disclosure gate + open-conflict panel (Stage 9); both frontiers flatten (`compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:690-700`; `compare_gemini_dr.txt:541`). |
| **Refusal / UNREACHABLE honesty** | **BEAT** | Fail-closed `abort_no_verified_sections` (Stage 10/O2); neither frontier ever refuses (`compare_gemini_q1.md`). MedHallu: abstention raises precision up to 15% (`clinical_rag_sota_deepest_research_2026_05_27.md:38`). |
| **Clinical-channel coverage** | **PARITY (must hold)** | New first-class PubMed E-utilities + ClinicalTrials.gov v2 backends + multi-round search (Stages 3-4) reach ChatGPT's full-SURPASS coverage (`compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:67-576`); shipped fetch-40 corpus is thinner. |
| **Depth (numeric specificity)** | **PARITY (must hold)** | Map-reduce supplies enough verified rows to surface exact ETDs/CIs (`compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:189-198`) — but only when a span supports them verbatim. |
| **Breadth (high-tier sources)** | **PARITY** | Relevance-driven corpus keeps ≥ Gemini's ~50 sources, with dedup-by-finding exposing rehash inflation. |
| **Prose readability** | **PARITY (real risk)** | DeepSeek writer must match Gemini's genuinely strong prose (`compare_gemini_q3.md:85`); this is the largest match-risk. |
| **Length / exhaustiveness** | **DELIBERATE-BEHIND** | 4,830-9,289 words is a clinical liability, not a target (`feedback_frontier_dr_not_agentic_2026_05_09`); POLARIS reports its saturation ceiling instead. |
| **Long-term / cross-session memory** | **DELIBERATE-BEHIND** | Out of scope for the single-shot golden-5 benchmark; not contested. |

---

## 4. WHAT CHANGES VS SHIPPED (ordered build list)

Ordered by dependency + leverage. Sizes are rough engineering estimates; all parameters config-driven (LAW VI). Items marked **wire-on** reuse existing code on the default path; **new** needs net-new code.

1. **Kill both count-caps → relevance + tier FLOORS + `corpus_funnel` manifest** (wire-on/selection-logic, ~1 day). Remove `max_rows` truncation in `select_evidence_for_generation` (`evidence_selector.py:976`); replace `chosen[:fetch_cap]` (`live_retriever.py:1573-1591`) with a relevance/tier floor; emit the binding funnel block. Closes the SILENT-dump half of the central loophole and is the prerequisite that lets every later stage see the full corpus.
2. **Default-on the 4-role gate** (wire-on, ~1-2 days). Pass the transport into `run_one_query` on the CLI/UI/queue path (`:3940`; `pipeline_a_ui_adapter.py:253`; `actors.py:206`) so Mirror/Sentinel/Judge run by default. Highest single-item leverage — the gate already exists.
3. **Per-source MAP digest (`evidence_reducer`)** (new, ~3-4 days). One DeepSeek digest per kept source → structured claim record; embarrassingly parallel. Unbinds N from the context window.
4. **Dedup-to-FINDINGS + REDUCE synthesis re-point** (new + wire-on, ~3-4 days). Cluster digests (lexical-key fallback for the autonomous path per §8.4; embeddings gated); re-point `multi_section_generator` at findings.
5. **PICO query-gen + MeSH validation** (new, ~3-4 days). Writer PICO faceting + NCBI E-utilities MeSH validation; feed existing deterministic expanders; replace `query_decomposer.py` flat split (`:33-36,108-148`).
6. **First-class PubMed E-utilities + ClinicalTrials.gov v2 backends** (new, ~3-4 days). esearch/efetch + ctgov v2; promote from "well-served via Serper" (`domain_backends.py:7`) / "skipped" (`:452`).
7. **Multi-round gap-analysis loop + saturation stop** (new, ~2-3 days). Gap-analysis between rounds; saturation stop-rule (`corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md §4.6`); replace the fixed R6-cap-4 expansion (`run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1885-1891`).
8. **Research-plan contract (per-section evidence target + done-definition)** (new + wire-on, ~2 days). Machine-checkable `plan` block in the manifest; wire to Stage-2 editable UI (#459/#754) and the Stage-9 INCOMPLETE flag.
9. **Claim atomization + 5-enum Judge + qualitative-negation Sentinel pass + guarded citation-repair** (new, ~4-5 days). The Stage-8 additions over the existing binary strict_verify.
10. **Conflict-as-input + disclosure evaluation gate** (new, ~2-3 days). Close the inert-`contradictions.json` loop into generation and evaluation; open-conflict panel (#454).
11. **Per-claim ledger + signed audit bundle + Proof-Replay split-view** (new + wire-on, ~4-5 days). O1/O3/O4 over existing UI components (#448-454, #461) and signed-manifest infra.

**Already invariant (keep ON at scale, no new code):** two-family segregation (§9.1.1), per-sentence provenance tokens (§9.1.2), python strict_verify floor (§9.1.3), zero-verified abort + corpus-approval abort (§9.3), delimiter sanitization (§9.1.7).

---

## 5. RUNTIME + COST

**Lineup confirmed** (`role_selection_final.md:32-35`): Writer `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`, Mirror `z-ai/glm-5.1`, Sentinel `ibm-granite/granite-guardian-4.1-8b` (self-host), Judge `qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b`. Four distinct families; all slugs/licenses/prices VERIFIED against the live OpenRouter catalog and named leaderboards (`role_selection_final.md:83`).

**Cost — two figures, reconciled honestly (do not conflate):**
- **Per-run target ≈ $2.63** (the locked operating envelope per the project brief) — this is the per-single-question full-power run including the generator-token bill and amortized self-host GPU; it is the budget the design must hold under `PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN`.
- **Per golden-5 verifier API ≈ $0.62** (`role_selection_final.md:64`), i.e. ≈ $0.123/question API-only, **excluding** the amortized Sentinel self-host GPU hour (~$0.50/hr 1×24GB, batch-size-independent).

These are consistent: the $0.62 is the 4-role *API* line over five questions; the ≈$2.63 *per run* additionally carries the Writer synthesis-token bill (trivial, <$2.30 even at N=1,500, `corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md:69`) plus the amortized GPU. Cost rises smoothly and monotonically with kept-N — extract $ linear in N, synth $ sub-linear because synthesis is over digests not full text (the point of map-reduce). All per-run dollar figures are [ESTIMATE] built on [VERIFIED] unit prices; only the on-disk baseline ($0.747686 / 22.18 min / 107 claims) is [MEASURED] (`corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md:69`).

**The one biggest cost/runtime risk:** the **EU-sovereign 4-role verifier wall-clock at scale** is the real `f(N)` and is **unmeasured at N≥200** (`corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md:16`); the Sentinel self-host GPU is the unbudgeted line (GPU spend is plausibly ~$250-920, not the ~$0.62 API figure, per `project_complete_solution_codex_corrections_2026_05_30`). The first full-scale run MUST measure both verifier wall-clock and Sentinel calls/run via the `corpus_funnel` + timing instrumentation before the $2.63 envelope is treated as confirmed.

---

## 6. PLAIN-SPOKEN SUMMARY (for a blind operator)

1. Today POLARIS searches once, keeps only 40 sources, and only about a third of its sentences are actually backed by a citation — that loses on coverage to the frontier tools.
2. Full-power searches in several rounds until it stops finding anything new, keeps every relevant source instead of the first 40, and reads each source on its own so none gets drowned out.
3. It writes evidence-first: it only writes a sentence if a real source already supports it, and it tags every sentence with the exact span it came from.
4. Then four different AI models check the work — one for over-confidence, one purpose-built hallucination catcher, one final judge, plus a strict math check that every number appears word-for-word in its source; anything that fails is dropped or refused, not smoothed over.
5. The frontier tools never refuse and never let you click a number to see its proof — Gemini's compute report had 4,932 words and zero citations; ChatGPT's footnotes point to nothing you can check. POLARIS makes every number one click from its source and shows you what it threw out.
6. We win on trustworthy, checkable answers — not on length or memory, which we deliberately don't chase; nothing is proven until one real blinded run on the five clinical questions scores it.

---

## 7. HONESTY FOOTER

**Grounded (on disk / verified):** the frontier weaknesses (Gemini zero-citation q1 `compare_gemini_q1.md`; ChatGPT stacked footnotes `compare_chatgpt_dr.txt:1067-1075` and opaque tokens `compare_chatgpt_q1.md:11`; both flatten conflicts and never refuse); the shipped caps and their line numbers (FETCH_CAP=40 `:1627`; MAX_EV_TO_GEN=20 `:2266`; gate-not-default-on `:3940`); the locked lineup, licenses, and unit prices (`role_selection_final.md:32-35,83`); the existing invariants (two-family, provenance tokens, strict_verify, fail-closed — CLAUDE.md §9). The redesign shape (relevance/tier floors + map-reduce) is the correct cure for rank-position truncation (`corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md:2`).

**Projected (not yet built / not yet measured):** the relevance-driven corpus, map-reduce, saturation stop-rule, default-on 4-role gate, PICO/MeSH query-gen, new clinical backends, and the audit-bundle/Proof-Replay surface are **designed, not built**; the per-run cost envelope, verifier wall-clock at N≥200, and Sentinel GPU spend are estimates on verified unit prices; every BEAT in §3 is a hypothesis until a blinded golden-5 run scores it claim-by-claim (RACE/FACT) with coverage ≥0.70.

**The single biggest unproven assumption:** that ~120-200 of ~1,000 discovered sources are genuinely relevant high-quality and ~800 are true noise (`corpus_relevance_driven_logic.md:91`). It is extrapolated from TREC-2020 Precision-Medicine top-rank precision (~29%), NOT measured on POLARIS's own pool — the only on-disk run had 24 rows. If true density is higher, kept-N, claim count, GPU-hours, wall-clock, and cost all slide up the same smooth `f(N)` curve (the model does not break, the numbers move). The first full-scale run MUST replace this prior with ground truth via the `corpus_funnel` manifest before any "beat" is claimed.

1088: cardiovascular safety assessment. Switch studies are becoming important because many patients are now
1089: already taking a GLP-1 receptor agonist before tirzepatide is considered. SURPASS-SWITCH, reported in
1090: 2026, found that switching from dulaglutide to tirzepatide produced greater glycemic and weight
1091: improvements than escalating dulaglutide. ClinicalTrials.gov also lists ongoing or recently active adult type

1: # CHATGPT Deep Research - Q1
2: 
3: **Question:** What is the cost-quality-jurisdiction trade-off between Canada operating its own sovereign frontier-LLM compute (SCALE-AI funded clusters, Quebec hydro) versus relying on US-headquartered hyperscalers (Azure, AWS, GCP) for federal-government AI workloads in 2026?
4: 
5: ---
6: 
7: Sovereign Frontier-LLM Compute Versus Hyperscaler Reliance for Federal AI Workloads in 2026\\
8: \\
9: ## Executive summary\\
10: \\
11: For Canadian federal AI workloads in 2026, the central trade-off is not simply \\\\u201ccheap sovereign compute\\\\u201d versus \\\\u201cexpensive cloud.\\\\u201d The real trade-off is **high-fixed-cost sovereignty with lower marginal cost** versus **high-variable-cost hyperscale access with better frontier quality, faster deployment, and richer managed controls**. Canada\\\\u2019s sovereign-compute push is now concrete: the \\\\ue200entity\\\\ue202[\\\\\"organization\\\\\",\\\\\"Government of Canada\\\\\",\\\\\"federal government of Canada\\\\\"]\\\\ue201 has committed up to C$700 million for private-sector compute capacity, up to C$1 billion for public supercomputing, and up to C$300 million for an AI Compute Access Fund; the AI Sovereign Compute Infrastructure Program alone will provide about C$890 million to the build layer beginning in fiscal 2026\\\\u201327. \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn31view0\\\\ue202turn31view1\\\\ue202turn31view2\\\\ue201\\
12: \\
13: On cost, a **nationally pooled sovereign cluster** can be materially cheaper than public-cloud list pricing **only when utilization is kept high and the fixed operating burden is shared across many users**. In the model used here, a 1,024-GPU H100/H200-class Canadian cluster amortized over five years lands at roughly **US$2.8/GPU-hour at 80% utilization**, but rises to **US$5.5/GPU-hour at 40% utilization**. Small enclaves are much more expensive because staffing, compliance, spares, and idle capital dominate. Against public examples retrieved for 2026, that means sovereign wins clearly against on-demand H100 cloud, but is much closer against committed/reserved capacity. \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn33view0\\\\ue202turn9search8\\\\ue202turn16search4\\\\ue202turn16search5\\\\ue202turn12search2\\\\ue202turn12search3\\\\ue201\\
14: \\
15: On performance and quality, the hyperscalers still have the upper hand. \\\\ue200url\\\\ue202Amazon Web Services\\\\ue202https://aws.amazon.com\\\\ue201 publicly documents H100, H200, B200, and GB200-era EC2 families; \\\\ue200url\\\\ue202Microsoft Azure\\\\ue202https://azure.microsoft.com\\\\ue201 documents H100, H200, MI300X, GB200, and GB300 VM families plus confidential H100 GPU options; and \\\\ue200url\\\\ue202Google Cloud\\\\ue202https://cloud.google.com\\\\ue201 documents H100, H200, and B200 classes with specialized AI Hypercomputer networking. Canada\\\\u2019s sovereign programs are substantial, but they are still in ramp-up, and one of the few public sovereign-factory examples in \\\\ue200entity\\\\ue202[\\\\\"state\\\\\",\\\\\"Quebec\\\\\",\\\\\"province in Canada\\\\\"]\\\\ue201\\\\u2014a \\\\ue200url\\\\ue202TELUS\\\\ue202https://www.telus.com\\\\ue201 facility in Rimouski\\\\u2014said in May 2026 that its first sovereign AI factory was already fully sold out. \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn8search0\\\\ue202turn8search1\\\\ue202turn39search0\\\\ue202turn39search6\\\\ue202turn39search14\\\\ue202turn15search0\\\\ue202turn15search1\\\\ue202turn15search4\\\\ue202turn32search3\\\\ue202turn32search19\\\\ue201\\
16: \\
17: On jurisdiction, **Canadian data residency is not the same thing as Canadian legal sovereignty**. Government guidance allows Protected B workloads in public cloud with Canadian data residency and cloud guardrails, but the government\\\\u2019s own white paper says limiting storage to Canada does not ensure full sovereignty over data. For U.S.-headquartered providers, the residual exposure comes from the U.S. provider nexus: the CLOUD Act enables U.S. legal process against U.S.-based providers regardless of storage location, and FISA Section 702 remains relevant to foreign-intelligence collection involving non-U.S. persons abroad. A Canadian-owned and Canadian-governed sovereign cluster does not eliminate lawful foreign-access risk altogether, because Canada can still execute requests through its Mutual Legal Assistance in Criminal Matters Act, but it does **shift control, audit rights, and legal process toward Canadian institutions**. \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn42search1\\\\ue202turn42search2\\\\ue202turn20search0\\\\ue202turn20search6\\\\ue202turn20search12\\\\ue202turn19search2\\\\ue202turn19search12\\\\ue202turn19search14\\\\ue202turn42search9\\\\ue201\\
18: \\
19: The procurement answer for 2026 is therefore not \\\\u201call sovereign\\\\u201d or \\\\u201call hyperscaler.\\\\u201d The best default is a **hybrid sovereign-core architecture**: keep the most sensitive model weights, fine-tuning corpora, evaluation pipelines, and long-running production inference on Canadian-governed infrastructure; use existing federal cloud contracts for burst, experimentation, and access to the newest accelerators. That recommendation aligns with the government\\\\u2019s cloud-first policy, its requirement for exit strategies and portability, and its expanding digital-sovereignty framing that goes beyond where bits are stored to who controls operations and resilience. \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn29view1\\\\ue202turn42search9\\\\ue202turn42search11\\\\ue202turn30search0\\\\ue201\\
20: \\
21: ## Market baseline and comparative table\\
22: \\
23: Canada\\\\u2019s sovereign-compute market is now shaped by two facts that push in opposite directions. First, the sovereign-compute strategy is large enough to matter: public supercomputing, private-sector AI data-centre buildout, and access funding are all being capitalized at national scale, with explicit requirements for Canadian location, governance, operational control, and reduced lock-in. Second, the cost of power and the scarcity of immediately available domestic capacity are becoming more binding. \\\\ue200url\\\\ue202Hydro-Qu\\\\u00e9bec\\\\ue202https://www.hydroquebec.com\\\\ue201 proposed in February 2026 that all new data centres over 5 MW in \\\\ue200entity\\\\ue202[\\\\\"state\\\\\",\\\\\"Quebec\\\\\",\\\\\"province in Canada\\\\\"]\\\\ue201 move to a new average rate of about **13\\\\u00a2/kWh**, approximately double what current large-power customers pay, and said data-centre electricity use in the province could rise sevenfold by 2035 to more than 1,000 MW. \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn31view1\\\\ue202turn31view2\\\\ue202turn33view0\\\\ue201\\
24: \\
25: That matters because the sovereign option is strongest only when federal demand is **aggregated**. A fragmented \\\\u201cmini-cluster in every department\\\\u201d strategy would create poor utilization and expensive security overhead. By contrast, the hyperscaler option benefits from existing Government of Canada cloud contracts: Treasury Board\\\\u2019s cloud-guardrail material says Protected B supply contracts were established with \\\\ue200url\\\\ue202Amazon Web Services Canada\\\\ue202https://aws.amazon.com\\\\ue201 and \\\\ue200url\\\\ue202Microsoft Canada\\\\ue202https://www.microsoft.com/en-ca\\\\ue201 in August 2019 and with \\\\ue200url\\\\ue202Google Cloud Canada\\\\ue202https://cloud.google.com\\\\ue201 in December 2022. That sharply lowers the contracting friction of a cloud or hybrid path. \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn30search0\\\\ue202turn42search11\\\\ue202turn29view1\\\\ue201\\
26: \\
27: | Option | Cost position in 2026 | Frontier quality and Canada-region reality | Jurisdictional risk for federal workloads | Compliance / security posture | Evidence |\\
28: |---|---|---|---|---|---|\\

Power is no longer ultra-cheap for new \u003e5 MW sites if the proposed 13\\\\u00a2/kWh tariff applies. | Node-level quality can match cloud **if the same silicon and full InfiniBand/NVLink fabric are procured**, but refresh cadence and spare-capacity depth are weaker. Existing public sovereign examples remain scarce; one early example said it was already sold out. | **Low to medium.** Best option for Canadian governance, audit rights, admin control, and residency. 30: | \\\\ue200url\\\\ue202Microsoft Azure\\\\ue202https://azure.microsoft.com\\\\ue201 | Publicly indexed 2026 retail snapshots for **ND96isr H100 v5** show roughly **US$98.32/h in East US** and **US$117.98/h in Canada Central**, or about **US$12.29\\\\u201314.75/GPU-hour**. Exact official list pricing was not directly retrievable in the official pricing pages used here. | Very strong accelerator breadth: H100, H200, MI300X, GB200, GB300, plus confidential H100 GPU options. Canada-region public H100 availability was less transparent in retrieved official docs than pricing mirrors suggest. | **Medium to high.** Canada-region residency helps, but U.S. corporate nexus remains. | CCCS Medium and Canada Protected B mapping, PBMM initiative content, ISO 27001, confidential VMs, and Managed HSM support a strong control story. | \\\\ue200cite\\\\ue202turn12search1\\\\ue202turn12search2\\\\ue202turn12search3\\\\ue202turn39search0\\\\ue202turn39search6\\\\ue202turn39search14\\\\ue202turn23search0\\\\ue202turn23search6\\\\ue202turn24search0\\\\ue202turn24search3\\\\ue202turn25search11\\\\ue201 |\\

 succeeded in 1201ms:
45: enzymatic degradation, extending the molecule's mean half-life to approximately five days, 
46: thereby enabling a highly convenient once-weekly subcutaneous dosing regimen.1 
47: Unlike native incretins or early combination attempts, tirzepatide functions as an "imbalanced" 
48: dual agonist. It exhibits an affinity for the GIP receptor that is comparable to that of native GIP, 
49: ensuring robust activation of this pathway. Conversely, its affinity for the GLP-1 receptor is 
50: significantly weaker than that of native GLP-1.1 This imbalanced profile is highly deliberate and 
51: clinically vital; it leverages the massive insulinotropic potential of GIP while utilizing just enough 
52: GLP-1 agonism to suppress glucagon and delay gastric emptying without overwhelming the 
53: receptor and causing intolerable early gastrointestinal distress. 
54: The physiological consequences of this dual agonism have been quantified in sophisticated 
55: mechanistic studies. In hyperinsulinemic-euglycemic clamp studies conducted over 28 weeks, 
56: the administration of tirzepatide at its maximum therapeutic dose of 15 mg increased 
57: whole-body insulin sensitivity by a remarkable 63%, as measured by the M-value.2 This massive 
58: reduction in peripheral insulin resistance is largely attributed to the profound reduction in 
59: visceral and ectopic fat depots driven by the drug's anorexigenic effects. Simultaneously, 
60: hyperglycemic clamp studies in patients with type 2 diabetes demonstrated that tirzepatide 15 
61: mg significantly enhanced both the first-phase and second-phase insulin secretion rates, 
62: effectively restoring the natural biphasic insulin response that is characteristically blunted or 
63: lost early in the pathogenesis of type 2 diabetes.2 
64: Furthermore, the dual agonism exerts potent central effects. By stimulating receptors in the 
65: hindbrain and hypothalamus, tirzepatide decreases food intake while simultaneously 
66: preventing the compensatory decrease in energy expenditure that typically accompanies 
67: extreme caloric restriction.3 This specific synergy is what produces weight reductions that 
68: significantly exceed the therapeutic ceiling of selective GLP-1 receptor mono-agonists.3 By 
69: directly reducing the beta-cell workload through profound weight loss while simultaneously 
70: amplifying the functional capacity of the remaining beta-cells via dual incretin receptor

7:I've completed your research. Feel free to ask me follow-up questions or request changes.Canada AI Compute: Sovereign vs. HyperscalerThe 2026 AI Compute Trilemma: Navigating Cost, Quality, and Jurisdiction in Canada's Federal AI WorkloadsIntroduction: The Sovereign Compute Imperative in 2026As artificial intelligence transitions from a frontier experimental capability to a foundational element of public infrastructure, the Canadian federal government is confronting a profound architectural and policy trilemma. The rapid deployment of advanced large language models (LLMs) requires infrastructure that simultaneously satisfies rigorous data sovereignty requirements, delivers world-class computational performance, and remains economically viable in an era of escalating energy and hardware costs. In 2026, this dynamic is encapsulated by the strategic choice between cultivating a domestic, sovereign AI compute ecosystem—anchored by massive federal initiatives like the Artificial Intelligence Sovereign Compute Infrastructure Program (SCIP) and industry clusters like SCALE AI—versus leveraging the immense scale, ecosystem maturity, and localized cryptographic adaptations of United States-headquartered hyperscalers such as Microsoft Azure, Amazon Web Services (AWS), and Google Cloud Platform (GCP).The stakes in 2026 extend far beyond the mechanics of public procurement; they encompass national security, economic competitiveness, and the absolute preservation of domestic intellectual property. The Canadian government has explicitly designated digital sovereignty as a paramount objective, aiming to exercise autonomy over digital infrastructure, proprietary data, and critical technologies while aggressively insulating federal operations from extraterritorial legal frameworks. Concurrently, the global demand for AI compute has reached staggering proportions. By the end of 2025, total global AI data center power capacity exceeded 30 gigawatts—a figure comparable to the peak power usage of entire industrialized states such as New York, and substantially larger than the baseline demand of many developed nations. Within this constrained and fiercely competitive global market, Canada has launched a $2 billion Sovereign AI Compute Strategy to secure domestic capacity, committing approximately $890 million to the Infrastructure Build Layer of SCIP alone.   However, global hyperscalers have not remained static in the face of these protectionist shifts. Recognizing the vulnerability of their lucrative public sector market share to rising data sovereignty concerns, firms like Microsoft have countered with historic investments. Microsoft's unprecedented $19 billion commitment to Canada introduces localized cloud controls, confidential computing enclaves, and contractual legal shields designed to seamlessly mimic the benefits of physical sovereignty while retaining global cloud agility. Furthermore, the fundamental economic foundations of Canadian computing are undergoing a paradigm shift. Historically heavily reliant on abundant, inexpensive hydroelectric power, jurisdictions like Quebec are sharply increasing tariffs for energy-intensive AI data centers to manage grid stability and protect residential consumers. This pricing evolution fundamentally alters the unit economics of domestic model training, eroding the long-standing geographic arbitrage that previously favored Canadian deployments.   This comprehensive report exhaustively analyzes the cost-quality-jurisdiction trade-off inherent in Canada's federal AI compute strategy. By meticulously examining the legal mechanisms of extraterritorial data access under United States law, the shifting economics of frontier hardware like the NVIDIA B200 Blackwell architecture, the severe impact of provincial power rate adjustments, and the software stack maturity of competing computing ecosystems, this analysis provides a definitive framework for optimizing federal AI workloads across strict Protected B and Protected C security classifications.The Jurisdictional Imperative and the Architecture of Data SovereigntyThe foundational tension driving the federal compute procurement debate is the definition, enforcement, and technological realization of data sovereignty. For the Government of Canada, digital sovereignty is not merely a political aspiration but a strict, legally binding operational requirement, particularly governed by bodies such as Shared Services Canada (SSC) and informed by legislative updates including Bill C-26 and the Critical Cyber Systems Protection Act. The classification of federal data dictates the stringency of these sovereignty controls. Protected B encompasses information that could cause serious injury to an individual or government organization if compromised, such as detailed financial information or medical records. Protected C represents the highest tier of non-classified sensitivity, encompassing information that could cause extremely grave injury, including national security intelligence, advanced defense logistics, and highly sensitive diplomatic communications.   The Extraterritorial Reach of the US CLOUD ActThe primary catalyst for Canada's aggressive sovereign compute push is the United States Clarifying Lawful Overseas Use of Data (CLOUD) Act of 2018. The CLOUD Act fundamentally altered the international data jurisdiction landscape by establishing that United States law enforcement authorities can compel US-based service providers to disclose data regardless of where that data is physically stored around the globe. For federal planners, this legislation laid bare the distinction between mere data residency and genuine data sovereignty.   The application of the CLOUD Act to Canadian data operates through three distinct and pervasive legal pathways. The first is direct application; when Canadian entities utilize services provided directly by US-headquartered corporations, the data is subject to US jurisdiction via the parent company's geographic domicile. The second, and most legally complex, is application through corporate control. The CLOUD Act's "possession, custody, or control" doctrine extends the legislation's reach to data held by foreign subsidiaries of US companies. This means that a Canadian-incorporated subsidiary of a global hyperscaler is still legally bound to comply with United States judicial orders, rendering local incorporation an insufficient shield. The third pathway involves application to foreign companies with a US presence; any service provider with a sufficient operational footprint in the US, regardless of its global headquarters, may be subject to the Act's sweeping provisions.   For federal departments operating under the mandate of Shared Services Canada (SSC), this sweeping extraterritoriality creates a severe and unacceptable compliance vulnerability. The SSC has explicitly stated in policy white papers that reliance on hyperscale cloud service providers (CSPs) with foreign operations means Canada simply cannot ensure full sovereignty over its data. This exposes sensitive government assets to foreign laws and potential disclosure to a foreign government, potentially without the Government of Canada's prior knowledge or consent.   This is not a hypothetical academic risk. It is the operational reality of utilizing foreign infrastructure. When a senior Microsoft executive was asked under oath before the French Senate in June 2025 whether he could guarantee that French government data stored in Microsoft's cloud would never be transmitted to US authorities without French authorization, his answer was unequivocal: "Non, je ne peux pas le garantir" ("No, I cannot guarantee it"). The identical legal reality applies to Canadian federal data. Storing data on Canadian soil does not automatically protect it; the CLOUD Act compels disclosure based on who ultimately controls the data, not strictly where the server racks are physically bolted down.   While data residency ensures physical storage within Canada, the US CLOUD Act asserts jurisdiction based on corporate control. True digital sovereignty requires the infrastructure operator to be exclusively subject to Canadian law, insulating data from extraterritorial legal demands. The architectural distinction is stark: a hyperscaler framework permits a foreign legal order to bypass physical geographic borders via corporate parent-subsidiary hierarchies, whereas a fully sovereign architecture halts such jurisdictional overreach directly at the national border due to independent, air-gapped domestic ownership. Encryption at rest, while an industry-standard security baseline, does not mitigate this specific jurisdictional risk if the cloud provider retains the ability to access the decryption keys or view the decrypted data during active computational processing.   The Hyperscaler Response: Cryptographic Sovereignty and Market DefenseRecognizing the existential threat that strict data sovereignty mandates pose to their massive public sector market share, United States hyperscalers have heavily criticized policies that conflate geographic server location with genuine security and resilience. Prominent think tanks and industry analysts argue that the risk of foreign legal orders is a theoretical extremity that ignores the practical, empirical reality of modern cloud security. Public transparency reports published by AWS, Microsoft, and Google indicate zero instances of Canadian enterprise or government content being disclosed to US law enforcement under the CLOUD Act, despite tens of thousands of routine global law enforcement demands.   To address the jurisdictional gap without fracturing their highly integrated global network architectures, hyperscalers have pioneered a concept that can be termed "cryptographic sovereignty." In 2026, Microsoft launched its Sovereign AI Landing Zone (SAIL) in Canada, backed by an unprecedented $19 billion capital investment pledge. This advanced architecture utilizes Azure Confidential Compute, a technology that keeps data strictly encrypted and isolated at the hardware level even while it is actively being processed in memory by AI models. Coupled with external key management via Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) that remain entirely under the Canadian client's control, hyperscalers assert that they are mathematically and physically incapable of complying with a foreign subpoena because they lack the cryptographic means to access the plaintext data, thereby neutralizing the CLOUD Act's reach.   Furthermore, hyperscalers have instituted rigorous organizational and contractual controls tailored specifically for the Canadian federal government. Google Cloud's Data Boundary for the Government of Canada ensures that technical support for Protected B workloads is provided exclusively by Canadian personnel who have completed extensive Reliability Status security screenings. The supported cloud services have successfully completed the Canadian Centre for Cyber Security (CCCS) rigorous assessment to support Protected B Medium (PBMM) and Protected B High Value Asset (PBHVA) workloads.   Amazon Web Services (AWS) offers similar verifiable controls over data access, combined with strict, codified contractual commitments to legally challenge any inappropriate or overbroad foreign data requests. AWS emphasizes that the CLOUD Act does not prohibit technical measures and operational controls designed to prevent access to customer data, asserting that their technical controls create multiple layers of protection that operate independently of corporate jurisdiction.   Despite these highly advanced technical mitigations, cryptographic sovereignty remains a heavily debated substitute for absolute legal sovereignty. As long as the physical infrastructure and underlying hypervisor are operated by a United States entity, theoretical attack vectors—or unprecedented extraterritorial legal maneuvers compelling the alteration of the host operating environment itself—remain a nonzero risk. For the most critical Protected C AI workloads, where the risk tolerance is functionally zero, this dependency renders the hyperscaler approach highly contentious.   The Domestic Ecosystem: SCALE AI and the Sovereign Compute StrategyTo establish a definitive, legally impregnable alternative to foreign CSPs, the Government of Canada initiated the $2 billion Canadian Sovereign AI Compute Strategy. This strategy is an aggressive, interventionist industrial policy designed to rectify the systemic underinvestment in domestic infrastructure that previously forced promising Canadian AI startups to incorporate abroad or rely entirely on United States infrastructure to achieve scale.   The AI Sovereign Compute Infrastructure Program (SCIP)The centerpiece of this federal strategy is the AI Sovereign Compute Infrastructure Program (SCIP), a highly structured initiative designed to construct a massive, publicly accessible AI supercomputer. Backed by approximately $890 million directed explicitly toward the Infrastructure Build Layer, SCIP represents a coordinated national effort to repatriate compute capacity and anchor a secure ecosystem.   A critical element of SCIP is its uncompromising sovereignty criteria, explicitly drafted to counter the CLOUD Act. Section 6 of the program guidelines strictly mandates that core compute and storage infrastructure must be owned or contractually controlled by Canadian entities, implementing robust safeguards to ensure no foreign party can unilaterally restrict use or demand access. The program insists that Canadian data, including highly sensitive datasets, must have appropriate protections while being leveraged to build AI solutions that directly address Canadian needs.   The SCIP architecture is deliberately bifurcated into two integrated operational layers to ensure the hardware is effectively utilized by the broader innovation ecosystem:The Infrastructure Build Layer: This component is responsible for the physical design, construction, systems administration, hardware operations, and long-term data center management.   The National Service Layer: This layer is tasked with transforming "bare metal" hardware into a highly usable platform. This includes providing the user interface, extensive APIs, AI and machine learning compute-as-a-service frameworks, active data management repositories, and seamless integration with Canada's existing Digital Research Infrastructure (DRI) ecosystem.   The Role of SCALE AI and Digital ClustersComplementing the raw infrastructure build is the deployment of specialized funding and industry integration through national clusters. Scale AI, Canada's AI-Powered Supply Chains Cluster based in Montréal, operates under a co-investment model that bridges the gap between Canada's world-class AI research and its commercial deployment. By late 2025, Scale AI announced a historic, record-breaking $128.5 million CAD funding round to support 44 new applied AI projects, driving collective investments toward the $500 million mark.   The critical sovereignty aspect of Scale AI's operations is its strict retention of intellectual property. As of March 2025, 100% of the IP created in the cluster's first 100 projects remained Canadian-owned, ensuring that the algorithmic innovations generated domestically are not immediately absorbed by foreign conglomerates. Projects funded range from automating municipal infrastructure management to complex port logistics optimization tools, demonstrating the broad utility of the ecosystem. Concurrently, the Vancouver-based Digital Global Innovation Cluster (DIGITAL) is advancing Canada's leadership in emerging digital technologies, injecting further capital into collaborative, industry-led projects.   Private Sector Alignment: TELUS and Alpha ComputeThe federal strategy is further amplified by massive private-sector investments explicitly aligning with the sovereign mandate. The telecommunications giant TELUS, in direct collaboration with the Government of Canada, has pioneered a network of Sovereign AI Factories. Following the rapid, unprecedented sell-out of its initial facility in Rimouski, Quebec, TELUS is executing a massive expansion into British Columbia with a cluster projected to scale to an astonishing 60,000 high-performance GPUs and 150 megawatts of power demand by 2032. This project represents a $9 billion economic injection and is explicitly marketed as a secure alternative to global hyperscalers. TELUS guarantees that intellectual property and sensitive data will never traverse outside Canadian borders, while running the entire operation on 98% clean hydroelectric energy and utilizing advanced liquid cooling with heat recovery technologies.   Additionally, highly agile domestic infrastructure firms like Alpha Compute have demonstrated the capability to deploy frontier hardware at speeds rivaling global tech giants. In early 2026, Alpha Compute officially activated the ALPHA-01 cluster in a Canadian datacenter, featuring 504 of the latest NVIDIA B200 GPUs, with aggressive plans to scale beyond 1,000 GPUs using specialized, hardware-backed non-recourse debt financing. These deployments comprehensively prove that Canadian sovereign clusters are not relegated to utilizing trailing-edge or outdated hardware; they are successfully securing tier-one supply chain allocations alongside global competitors.   The Cost Trade-Off: Hardware Economics, Power Tariffs, and the Energy ShockOperating sovereign frontier AI compute involves immense, multi-generational capital expenditure (CapEx) and deeply complex operational expenditure (OpEx). While the federal SCIP funding and private debt markets provide the initial CapEx necessary to secure hardware, the OpEx of Canadian infrastructure is undergoing a radical, state-driven transformation in 2026.The Quebec Hydro Rate Shock and the Erosion of ArbitrageHistorically, Canada's primary competitive advantage in global datacenter hosting was rooted in the province of Quebec's abundant, exceptionally low-cost, and sustainable hydroelectric power. However, the explosive, exponential growth of artificial intelligence has fundamentally altered grid dynamics. Global AI power capacity escalated to approximately 30 GW by late 2025, and Hydro-Québec internal projections indicate that datacenter electricity demand within the province will surge an astonishing sevenfold by 2035, exceeding 1,000 MW.   To prevent these colossal fixed infrastructure and grid expansion costs from being passed onto regular residential consumers—and to politically and economically extract the "full value" of the province's green energy premium—Hydro-Québec formally petitioned the provincial energy regulator (Régie de l'énergie) to double the electricity rates for new data centers exceeding 5 MW of capacity. Set to take effect in the second half of 2026, the new rate establishes a strict baseline of 13 cents per kilowatt-hour (kWh) for large AI data centers, while cryptographic and blockchain operations face an even steeper, punitive hike to an average of 19.5 cents/kWh.   This 13¢/kWh rate fundamentally reshapes the cross-border cost comparison. While it remains nominally lower than the United States national commercial average of 14.12¢/kWh, it severely erodes the historic energy arbitrage advantage against prime US datacenter hubs. For instance, Virginia—the densest and most mature datacenter market in the world—maintains average commercial electricity rates of approximately 11.44¢ to 13.5¢/kWh depending on specific load tiering, while Texas operates at highly competitive commercial rates near 10.7¢/kWh. By achieving cost-parity with the absolute core of the United States infrastructure market, Quebec's sovereign compute clusters can no longer compete strictly on lower operating margins. They must now justify their premium pricing through enhanced privacy, rigid security protocols, and ironclad jurisdictional guarantees.   Comparative Electricity Costs: Sovereign vs. Hyperscale HubsThe impact of grid economics can be quantified by comparing the prevailing rates in major geographic hosting zones. The table below illustrates the narrowing gap between Canadian and US power pricing.JurisdictionSector ClassRate (cents/kWh)Strategic Impact on AI WorkloadsQuebec (H2 2026)Large Data Center (>5 MW)13.00¢Eliminates historical arbitrage; forces competition on sovereignty value.Texas (May 2026)Commercial10.77¢Highly attractive for hyperscale builds due to deregulated energy market.Virginia (May 2026)Commercial11.44¢Intense cluster density maintains competitive rates despite massive demand.US National AvgCommercial14.12¢Represents the baseline operating cost for broadly distributed hyperscale zones.Quebec (Crypto)Blockchain/Crypto19.50¢Punitive rate designed to force out low-economic-benefit workloads.Export to SheetsFrontier Hardware: The Economics of the NVIDIA Blackwell B200Beyond foundational power constraints, the silicon hardware itself dictates the ultimate cost-quality equation. In 2026, the NVIDIA B200 Blackwell architecture has become the undisputed, indispensable standard for frontier AI workloads, completely eclipsing the previous generation's H100 Hopper chips. The B200 represents a monumental leap in hardware engineering, featuring 192 GB of HBM3e memory operating at an unprecedented 8 TB/s bandwidth, along with dedicated support for ultra-low precision FP4 and FP6 calculations, which the older H100 architecture lacks.   This colossal memory bandwidth is critical for the economics of LLM inference. For a standard 70-billion parameter model operating in FP16 precision, an H100 GPU can sustain an inference rate of approximately 24 tokens per second. In stark contrast, the B200 delivers roughly 57 tokens per second—a nearly 140% massive increase in raw throughput.   Consequently, while the B200 hardware commands a significant premium rental price—averaging $4.50 to $6.00 per hour for on-demand instances across global cloud providers, and soaring up to $9.86/hr depending on the cluster size and commitment duration—the ultimate cost-per-token generated actually plummets due to its vast efficiency gains.   ProviderHardware ProfilePricing ModelHourly Rate (per GPU)Market AvailabilityCoreWeaveNVIDIA B200Reserved$2.65Waitlisted / EnterpriseRunPodNVIDIA B200On-Demand$5.98ImmediateInworld ComputeNVIDIA B200On-DemandFrom $6.00Available NowLambdaNVIDIA B200 (16-node)On-Demand$9.862-week to 1-year termsIndustry AvgNVIDIA H100On-Demand$1.38 - $11.01Broad availabilityExport to SheetsFor the Canadian sovereign ecosystem, securing B200 availability is absolutely paramount for remaining technologically relevant. Global supply remains highly constrained, with an estimated 3.6 million units heavily backordered through mid-2026. Sovereign providers face profoundly higher up-front capital risks compared to hyper-capitalized global hyperscalers who have the financial leverage to purchase in massive, multi-billion dollar bulk allocations.   The AI Compute Access Fund and Federal Procurement ConstraintsTo alleviate the financial burden of these staggering hardware costs for domestic innovators, the Government of Canada deployed the AI Compute Access Fund. Managed under the Sovereign AI Compute Strategy, this $300 million fund is specifically designed to tackle the high cost of compute resources and the limited availability of domestic capacity. The fund operates as a direct market intervention, aggressively subsidizing compute costs for eligible Canadian Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs). The structure covers an impressive two-thirds of the eligible costs for utilizing Canadian, cloud-based AI compute services, while only covering half of the costs if the SME opts for a non-Canadian, foreign-hosted compute service.   This two-tiered subsidy explicitly drives commercial demand toward domestic sovereign clusters, artificially insulating local firms from the higher localized capital and energy costs. However, there is a critical, often overlooked constraint in this funding architecture: the eligibility criteria are exceptionally rigid. To access the fund, an entity must be a Canadian-registered for-profit company with fewer than 500 full-time employees, must possess a Canada-based R&D team, and must be actively revenue-generating or possess minimum Series A financing.   Therefore, federal government departments and agencies themselves are entirely ineligible for these specific SME subsidies. Consequently, federal IT procurement under Shared Services Canada must bear the unvarnished, true market rates of sovereign infrastructure. This reality makes the raw, un-subsidized cost delta between a Canadian-hosted B200 instance operating on 13¢/kWh power and a deeply optimized Azure or AWS B200 instance an acute, inescapable fiscal consideration for government planners attempting to balance tight departmental budgets.The Quality Gap: Infrastructure Provisioning versus Ecosystem MaturityIf the cost equation hinges heavily on power tariffs and hardware economics, the quality equation is defined almost entirely by the maturity of the software stack, the breadth of platform services, and developer velocity. Here, the trade-off between emerging domestic sovereign compute and entrenched US hyperscalers is most pronounced and challenging.The Hyperscaler Platform AdvantageHyperscalers do not merely rent out bare-metal GPUs attached to a network connection; they provide highly integrated, frictionless, and secure ecosystems of Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) tools. Amazon Web Services (AWS) offers Amazon Bedrock, a flagship, fully managed serverless service that grants developers immediate API access to a multitude of foundation models—including Anthropic's Claude, Meta's Llama, and Cohere—without requiring the user to provision or manage the underlying GPU hardware.   Microsoft Azure holds a massive structural advantage through its exclusive, multi-billion-dollar partnership with OpenAI, integrating cutting-edge generative AI directly into the ubiquitous Microsoft 365 ecosystem, allowing federal employees to leverage Copilot functionalities seamlessly. Google Cloud Platform (GCP) leverages its Vertex AI platform and deep integration with proprietary silicon (Tensor Processing Units, or TPUs) to power the Gemini model family, earning recognition as a Leader in the 2026 Forrester Wave for Sovereign Cloud Platforms.   These hyperscale ecosystems dramatically reduce the time-to-deployment for complex AI applications. Federal departments utilizing Azure or AWS benefit from automated container orchestration, continuous zero-trust security monitoring via specialized tools like Wiz's Security Graph, and seamless, massively scalable data pipelining. The genuine "quality" of a hyperscaler is rooted not just in the silicon, but in this unparalleled developer velocity, operational reliability, and the elimination of maintenance friction.   The Sovereign Software Challenge and the National Service LayerConversely, pure sovereign infrastructure projects, particularly in their nascent stages of deployment, face a significant risk of functioning merely as high-performance compute (HPC) collocation facilities that severely lack high-level orchestration software. A raw, unmanaged cluster of 1,000 advanced B200 GPUs is exceptionally powerful in theory, but without a mature software ecosystem, federal data scientists must expend vast, highly expensive engineering resources managing container orchestration, load balancing across nodes, model weight distribution, and vital security patching entirely manually.The Government of Canada has astutely recognized this severe vulnerability in its procurement strategy. The SCIP procurement process explicitly bifurcates funding, dedicating immense resources to the "National Service Layer" specifically to prevent the newly minted supercomputer from becoming an unusable, bare-metal silo. The winning National Service Layer provider is strictly mandated to develop the UI/UX portals, extensive APIs, comprehensive AI and machine learning compute-as-a-service frameworks, active data management repositories, and training support necessary to make the hardware accessible.   Furthermore, private sovereign initiatives are actively forging strategic partnerships to rapidly build this vital software stack. For example, Datavault AI has deeply integrated with CyberCatch to overlay continuous-compliance monitoring and automated cybersecurity defenses natively across domestic AI edge platforms. This creates a sovereign software ecosystem designed specifically to rival hyperscaler tooling in heavily regulated industries. Additionally, Microsoft's strategic integration of Cohere's made-in-Canada sovereign models into the Azure Foundry lineup demonstrates a pragmatic hybrid approach, where highly protected domestic intellectual property is deployed efficiently via global cloud platforms.   Ultimately, the quality trade-off in 2026 undeniably favors the hyperscalers for immediate, out-of-the-box application development and rapid deployment. However, the heavily funded sovereign National Service Layer is actively closing this capability gap, aiming to provide customized, highly secure, and functionally comparable environments optimized specifically for foundational model training and sensitive government inference tasks.Synthesizing the Trilemma for Federal Workloads in 2026The complex decision matrix for deploying federal government AI workloads in 2026 cannot be solved by a uniform, monolithic policy directive. The cost, quality, and jurisdiction variables intersect entirely differently depending on the specific data classification and risk profile of the workload in question. Federal IT strategists, operating under the guidance of Shared Services Canada, must rigorously evaluate the landscape across two primary operational vectors: the Protected B paradigm and the Protected C paradigm.The Protected B Paradigm: Leveraging Cryptographic SovereigntyProtected B data encompasses highly sensitive personal information, medical records, detailed financial data, and performance evaluation reports. The unauthorized disclosure or compromise of this data could reasonably be expected to cause serious organizational or individual injury, severe distress, financial loss, or significant damage to reputation. While highly sensitive, it generally does not rise to the existential level of national security or defense intelligence.   For Protected B AI workloads, the optimal trade-off in 2026 heavily favors leveraging the mature, localized hyperscaler ecosystems.Jurisdiction: Hyperscalers have successfully and demonstrably met the Government of Canada's stringent technical requirements for this tier. Google Cloud's Data Boundary for Canada and its successful, rigorous Canadian Centre for Cyber Security (CCCS) assessment for Protected B High Value Asset (PBHVA) workloads ensure that technical support is managed exclusively by cleared Canadian personnel and that the data physically resides locally within the nation's borders. Microsoft's aggressive deployment of the Sovereign AI Landing Zone (SAIL) and deep integration of Confidential Compute provides mathematical, cryptographic assurances that data remains completely inaccessible to foreign operators even during active, in-memory processing.   Quality: Utilizing Azure, AWS, or GCP allows federal departments to instantly leverage cutting-edge generative AI models, robust automated cybersecurity integrations—thereby radically reducing the "toxic cloud trilogy" of dangerous misconfigurations and identity exposures—and mature orchestration tools without suffering the severe latency of waiting for the domestic National Service Layer to fully mature and stabilize.   Cost: Hyperscalers absorb the massive, multi-billion-dollar capital risk of hardware procurement, supply chain logistics, and rapid technological refresh cycles. Federal departments benefit directly from highly optimized OpEx models and fiercely competitive on-demand B200 pricing, avoiding the punishing, un-subsidized localized capital costs and the 13¢/kWh energy premium that plague standalone sovereign builds.The theoretical risk of CLOUD Act exposure for Protected B data is deemed an acceptable operational risk by many framework analysts when comprehensively counterbalanced by robust cryptographic controls (like customer-managed HSMs), strictly codified contractual legal defiance clauses, and the unparalleled operational efficiency of hyperscale ecosystems.   The Protected C Paradigm: The Absolute Sovereign MandateProtected C data involves critical national security intelligence, advanced defense logistics, classified architectural schematics, and highly sensitive diplomatic communications. In this domain, the cost-quality-jurisdiction calculus inverses entirely.   For Protected C AI workloads, the optimal—and legally requisite—trade-off demands absolute reliance on true domestic sovereign infrastructure.Jurisdiction: The fundamental, classified nature of Protected C strictly prohibits the acceptance of any theoretical extraterritorial vulnerability. Cryptographic sovereignty is deemed entirely insufficient because the physical hardware, the underlying hypervisor, and the ultimate operational control remain vested in a foreign corporate entity subject to the "possession, custody, or control" doctrine of the United States CLOUD Act. As established by international precedent and explicit French Senate testimony, global hyperscalers cannot unconditionally guarantee absolute immunity from covert or unprecedented US law enforcement mandates. Therefore, Protected C workloads must reside on strictly air-gapped infrastructure that is owned, operated, and legally bound exclusively by Canadian entities. This is precisely the infrastructure envisioned by the SCIP program and bespoke, highly secured deployments by domestic telecommunications giants like TELUS.   Quality: While pure sovereign clusters may currently lack the expansive, user-friendly PaaS maturity of AWS Bedrock or Azure OpenAI, raw computational power is no longer a deficit. Domestic facilities like Alpha Compute are actively and successfully deploying 8 TB/s bandwidth Blackwell B200 architectures concurrently with major US deployments. Furthermore, for highly classified environments, federal data science teams typically build custom, heavily containerized environments regardless of the host, rendering the absence of commercial PaaS tools less detrimental. The forthcoming, heavily funded National Service Layer will further standardize and secure these sensitive workflows.   Cost: In the realm of Protected C data, the fiscal efficiency of the cloud is entirely subordinated to the imperatives of national security. The federal government must willingly absorb the substantial premium of sovereign CapEx and the operational friction of Quebec's elevated 13¢/kWh energy rates. This financial premium is the unavoidable, necessary price of absolute jurisdictional control and national digital independence.   ConclusionThe 2026 landscape of Canadian federal AI compute is defined by the profound tension between the immense gravitational pull of United States hyperscale efficiency and the indispensable, non-negotiable necessity of sovereign jurisdictional control. The Government of Canada has astutely recognized that neither path is universally superior, nor can a single architecture satisfy the vast, disparate needs of the modern federal apparatus.The $2 billion Sovereign AI Compute Strategy, underscored by the massive SCIP infrastructure build and the targeted SME support of the AI Compute Access Fund, successfully ensures that Canada will not be entirely dependent on foreign infrastructure for its most critical operations or the cultivation of its domestic innovation ecosystem. Simultaneously, the aggressive, localized investments by Microsoft, AWS, and Google—manifested in sophisticated sovereign landing zones, localized personnel constraints, and confidential computing enclaves—provide a highly secure, vastly efficient, and cryptographically sound pathway for the vast majority of standard government modernization efforts.   Ultimately, the resolution to the cost-quality-jurisdiction trilemma is a strategically bifurcated deployment architecture. For the immense volume of operational and Protected B federal workloads, utilizing localized hyperscaler architectures maximizes computational quality and operational velocity while maintaining robust, cryptographically enforced data residency. However, for Protected C datasets and classified national security intelligence, the uncompromising requirement for legal absolute sovereignty dictates exclusive reliance on Canada's emerging, Blackwell-equipped domestic sovereign compute clusters, accepting the structural and energy costs as a necessary investment in enduring national resilience and digital self-determination.
